perturbation.original_text: The Sponsor shall not be liable for any error of judgment or mistake of law or for any act or omission in the oversight, administration or management of the Trust or the performance of its duties hereunder, except for willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence in the performance of its duties, or by reason of the reckless disregard of its obligations and duties hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sponsor shall not be liable for any error of judgment or mistake of law or for any act or omission in the oversight, administration or management of the Trust or the performance of its duties hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'except for willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence in the performance of its duties, or by reason of the reckless disregard of its obligations and duties hereunder' clause, the agreement attempts to shield the Sponsor from liability even in cases of willful misconduct, bad faith, gross negligence, or reckless disregard of duties. This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, and the prohibition on exculpatory clauses for gross negligence or intentional misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law does not allow parties to contract away liability for bad faith or gross negligence. M.G.L. c. 156B, § 13 allows corporations to define officer liability, but it would be argued that a blanket waiver for actions, including bad faith, would be impermissible, especially given the fiduciary nature of the relationship. This law is being contradicted, as it limits the ability to remove liability for actions conducted in bad faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXII, Chapter 156B, Section 13 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXII CORPORATIONS Chapter 156B CERTAIN BUSINESS CORPORATIONS Section 13 Articles of organization; contents Section 13 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 13: Articles of organization; contents Section 13. (a) The articles of organization shall state: (1) that the incorporators (stating their names, including their given names, and post office addresses) associate themselves with the intention of forming a corporation; (2) the name of the corporation; (3) the purposes for which the corporation is formed; (4) the total number of shares and the par value, if any, of each class of stock which the corporation is authorized to issue; (5) if more than one class of stock is authorized, a distinguishing designation for each class and, prior to the issuance of any shares of a class, if shares of any other class are outstanding, a description of the preferences, voting powers, qualifications, and special or relative rights or privileges of that class and of each other class of which shares are outstanding, and of each series then established within any class. (b) The articles of organization, in addition, may state: (1) any restrictions imposed upon the transfer of shares of stock of any class; (11/2) a provision eliminating or limiting the personal liability of a director to the corporation or its stockholders for monetary damages for breach of fiduciary duty as a director notwithstanding any provision of law imposing such liability; provided, however, that such provision shall not eliminate or limit the liability of a director (i) for any breach of the director's duty of loyalty to the corporation or its stockholders, (ii) for acts or omissions not in good faith or which involve intentional misconduct or a knowing violation of law, (iii) under section sixty-one or sixty-two,
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'except' clause directly contradicts the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for bad faith or gross negligence, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, M.G.L. c. 156B, § 13, allows limiting director liability but specifically excludes liability for 'acts or omissions not in good faith or which involve intentional misconduct'. By removing the exception for willful misfeasance, bad faith, or gross negligence, the changed text attempts to shield the Sponsor from liability even in cases of intentional misconduct, which the law prohibits. This significantly weakens legal protections and attempts to create an exculpatory clause that is legally impermissible.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, with respect to any series or the Trust, by the Board of Trustees of the Trust, or by the Sponsor, in each case on not less than 60 days' written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, with respect to any series or the Trust, by the Sponsor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the Board of Trustees' right to terminate the agreement (or making it seemingly more difficult by implication), the agreement infringes upon their fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the Trust and its beneficiaries. Removing this right would create a legal issue as it would allow the Sponsor to continue the agreement even when it is detrimental to the beneficiaries. Massachusetts law requires that the board of trustees act in the best interest of the beneficiaries.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 203E, § 802 details the duty that the trustees have over the trust. A contract preventing their termination of a bad actor would be against the interest of the beneficiaries, and a potential violation of the trust as they would not be able to fulfil their fiduciary duties.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows both the Board of Trustees and the Sponsor to terminate the agreement with 60 days' notice, ensuring the Board can act in the best interest of the Trust. The changed text removes the Board of Trustees' explicit right to terminate, potentially hindering their ability to fulfill their fiduciary duty. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this removal could violate the trustees' fiduciary duties, particularly M.G.L. c. 203E, § 802, as they might be unable to terminate an agreement detrimental to the beneficiaries' interests. While direct legal snippets are missing, the explanations provided clearly outline how the change contradicts the fiduciary duty requirements. The omission of the Board's right creates a legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and enforced and interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts without reference to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and enforced and interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of a jurisdiction selected by the Sponsor.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from the Commonwealth of Massachusetts to a jurisdiction selected by the Sponsor allows the Sponsor to choose a jurisdiction with laws favorable to them, potentially undermining consumer protections afforded under Massachusetts law. This creates a legal risk because Massachusetts law has specific regulations protecting consumers and investors. Choosing a different jurisdiction could violate the rights of the Trust beneficiaries under Massachusetts law.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By potentially selecting a jurisdiction that does not provide similar consumer protection, the Sponsor could shield itself from liability under Chapter 93A, thus contradicting the intent and purpose of the Massachusetts law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce. By allowing the Sponsor to select a different jurisdiction's laws, the changed text potentially shields the Sponsor from the consumer protections afforded under Massachusetts law. This directly opposes the intent of Chapter 93A, Section 2(a), which declares unlawful 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce.' The ability to choose a more favorable legal environment undermines the protections Massachusetts law provides to consumers, creating a legal contradiction.
